summary: Wes McKinney frames his move to Posit as a continuation of long-standing collaboration aimed at building polyglot, open-source data tools. He argues that the real competition is open source versus proprietary software, not R versus Python, and that productive collaboration grows once language wars subside. He highlights persistent pain around cross-language packaging and deployment, pointing to tools like Conda, Mamba, and Pixi as partial remedies but not a full solution. On AI, he treats Copilot integration as table stakes while warning about ethical and workforce impacts, and he is interested in LLMs as research assistants for data exploration. His career advice centers on long-term relationship building and resilience to negative feedback in open source.
key_points: Posit’s role in incubating Arrow and related efforts shows the value of cross-language collaboration and shared infrastructure.
Packaging and deployment across Python and R remain major blockers; language-agnostic tools are necessary but still immature.
The most important strategic divide is open source versus closed source, not language rivalry.
LLM tooling should augment workflows beyond code generation, but raises ethical and labor concerns.
Sustainable careers in open source require long-term relationship investment and emotional resilience.
money_quotes: We’re not working R versus Python, we’re really open source versus closed source proprietary software.

Getting past the red team, blue team bickering about whose programming language is better has enabled us to have just a lot more productive conversations about how we can build real tools for humans that are usable and accessible.
The feedback that you get in an open-source project tends to only be negative feedback.
When somebody’s annoyed with your software not doing what they wish it did or being perfect in the ways that they wish for it to be perfect, that comment is as much about them as it is about you.
The downside of making developers more productive is in the future we need fewer developers, most likely.
I’ve made a personal promise to myself to never work on a Python packaging tool because we’ve all thought about it.
Focus on people and relationships, investing in your relationships with people that you want to work with for a long time and also viewing your working relationships with a long-term mindset.
key_insight: The long-term leverage in data tooling comes from open, interoperable infrastructure and sustained human relationships, not language tribalism.
